content:
# Joshua Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 236-237 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_013_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Pivot — Thirty-three verses that shift the entire book from conquest to inheritance. Joshua is old. The land is not fully taken. And yet YHWH says: divide it anyway. What follows is the great geographic inventory — a roll call of unconquered territories, then the detailed allotments east of the Jordan that Moses had already given to Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. This is the hinge chapter: the age of warfare yields to the age of settlement, and the land becomes promise-in-progress rather than prize already won. The lists are not tedious; they are titles of ownership, each place-name a deed of trust from YHWH to Israel. PNEUMA renders the geography plainly and lets the theological architecture speak: the land belongs to YHWH, and he assigns it as he sees fit — even the parts not yet conquered.]*

---

**1** Now Joshua was old, advanced in years. And YHWH said to him, "You are old, advanced in years, and a great deal of land still remains to be possessed.

**2** "This is the land that remains: all the regions of the Philistines and all the territory of the Geshurites — **3** from the Shihor east of Egypt to the border of Ekron on the north, which is counted as Canaanite — the five Philistine rulers of Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron — as well as the Avvites **4** to the south; all the land of the Canaanites; and Mearah that belongs to the Sidonians, as far as Aphek, to the border of the Amorites; **5** also the land of the Gebalites, and all of Lebanon toward the east, from Baal-Gad at the foot of Mount Hermon to Lebo-Hamath. **6** All the inhabitants of the hill country from Lebanon to Misrephoth-Maim — all the Sidonians — I myself will drive them out before the Sons of Israel. Only allot it to Israel as an inheritance, just as I have commanded you.

**7** "Now divide this land as an inheritance among the nine tribes and the half-tribe of Manasseh."

**8** With the other half-tribe, the Reubenites and the Gadites had already received the inheritance that Moses gave them east of the Jordan — just as Moses the servant of YHWH had assigned it to them: **9** from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon gorge, including the town in the middle of the gorge, and the whole tableland of Medeba as far as Dibon; **10** all the cities of Sihon king of the Amorites, who ruled in Heshbon, as far as the border of the Ammonites; **11** also Gilead, the territory of the Geshurites and Maacathites, all of Mount Hermon, and all of Bashan as far as Salecah — **12** the entire kingdom of Og in Bashan, who ruled in Ashtaroth and Edrei. He was the last survivor of the Rephaim. Moses had defeated them and driven them out. **13** But the Sons of Israel did not drive out the Geshurites or the Maacathites, and Geshur and Maacath live among Israel to this day.

**14** Only to the tribe of Levi he gave no inheritance. The fire offerings of YHWH the God of Israel are their inheritance, just as he told them.

**15** Moses gave an allotment to the tribe of the Sons of Reuben, clan by clan. **16** Their territory ran from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon gorge, including the town in the middle of the gorge, and the whole tableland by Medeba — **17** Heshbon and all its towns on the tableland: Dibon, Bamoth-Baal, Beth-Baal-Meon, **18** Jahaz, Kedemoth, Mephaath, **19** Kiriathaim, Sibmah, Zereth-Shahar on the hill in the valley, **20** Beth-Peor, the slopes of Pisgah, Beth-Jeshimoth — **21** all the towns of the tableland, and the entire kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites, who ruled in Heshbon. Moses had defeated him, along with the Midianite chiefs Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba — the vassal princes of Sihon who lived in the land. **22** Among those the Sons of Israel killed with the sword was Balaam son of Beor, the diviner. **23** The boundary of the Sons of Reuben was the Jordan and its floodplain. This was the inheritance of the Sons of Reuben, clan by clan — the towns and their settlements.

**24** Moses gave an allotment to the tribe of Gad, to the Sons of Gad, clan by clan. **25** Their territory included Jazer and all the towns of Gilead, and half the land of the Ammonites as far as Aroer, which faces Rabbah; **26** and from Heshbon to Ramath-Mizpeh and Betonim, and from Mahanaim to the border of Debir; **27** and in the valley: Beth-Haram, Beth-Nimrah, Succoth, and Zaphon — the rest of the kingdom of Sihon king of Heshbon — with the Jordan as a border, up to the edge of the Sea of Chinnereth on the east side of the Jordan. **28** This was the inheritance of the Sons of Gad, clan by clan — the towns and their settlements.

**29** Moses gave an allotment to the half-tribe of Manasseh. It was assigned to the half-tribe of the Sons of Manasseh, clan by clan. **30** Their territory ran from Mahanaim: all of Bashan — the entire kingdom of Og king of Bashan — and all the settlements of Jair in Bashan, sixty towns; **31** half of Gilead, and Ashtaroth and Edrei, the royal cities of Og in Bashan. These went to the Sons of Machir son of Manasseh — to half the Sons of Machir, clan by clan.

**32** These are the allotments that Moses distributed on the plains of Moab, east of the Jordan across from Jericho. **33** But to the tribe of Levi, Moses gave no inheritance. YHWH the God of Israel is their inheritance, just as he told them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "You are old, advanced in years" (zaqanta ba'ta ba-yamim):** YHWH's opening statement is startlingly blunt. The phrase ba'ta ba-yamim literally means "you have come into the days" — an idiom for extreme age. This is not a gentle observation; it is a strategic assessment. Joshua's mortality is the reason for urgency: the land must be divided now, before his death, even though it is not fully conquered. PNEUMA renders plainly and lets the directness land.

**V. 2-6 — The unconquered territory:** This is a remarkable catalog of what Israel has not taken. It includes the entire Philistine coast (five city-states), the Geshurite territory, the Phoenician coast up to Sidon, the Gebalite region (Byblos), and all of Lebanon. The list is honest — after the sweeping conquest narratives of chapters 10-12, the text now acknowledges how much remains. Yet YHWH says: divide it anyway. The promise precedes the possession.

**V. 3 — "The five Philistine rulers" (khamesheth sarne pelishthim):** The term seren (plural seranim) is used exclusively for Philistine rulers and may be related to the Greek tyrannos. It is a foreign loan-word that marks the Philistines as ethnically and politically distinct from Canaanite kings (melakhim). PNEUMA renders "rulers" to distinguish from the "kings" used elsewhere.

**V. 5 — "Lebo-Hamath" (levo' khamath):** This phrase marks the idealized northern boundary of the promised land. It means "the entrance to Hamath" — the pass leading into the territory of the kingdom of Hamath in modern Syria. It recurs throughout the Hebrew Bible as a geographic marker for the far northern limit of Israel's claim. PNEUMA transliterates rather than translates, following scholarly convention.

**V. 6 — "I myself will drive them out" ('anokhi orishem):** The emphatic first-person pronoun 'anokhi — YHWH claims the military task as his own. Israel's job is not to conquer the remaining territory but to allot it in faith. The driving out will be YHWH's work. This is the theological logic of the entire land-division: you divide what you do not yet hold, because the one who holds it all has spoken.

**V. 8 — "With the other half-tribe" ('immo):** The Hebrew is compressed. The pronoun "with him/it" connects back to the half-tribe of Manasseh mentioned in v. 7. The Reubenites and Gadites, along with the other half of Manasseh, had already received their inheritance from Moses east of the Jordan. PNEUMA clarifies the reference for readability.

**V. 12 — "The last survivor of the Rephaim" (mi-yether ha-repha'im):** Og of Bashan is identified as the remnant of the Rephaim — the legendary race of giants. The word yether means "remainder" or "survivor." Og's death thus marks the end of an era. The Rephaim tradition connects to Deuteronomy 3:11, which describes Og's enormous iron bed. PNEUMA renders "last survivor" to capture the finality.

**V. 13 — "Live among Israel to this day":** The etiological formula ('ad ha-yom ha-zeh) signals that the narrator writes from a later period when the Geshurites and Maacathites were still present among the Israelites. This is one of several such notes in Joshua that indicate incomplete conquest — the gap between divine promise and human reality.

**V. 14 — "The fire offerings of YHWH... are their inheritance" ('isheh YHWH... hu' nakhalatho):** The Levites receive no territorial allotment. Their "inheritance" is the sacrificial system itself — they are sustained by the offerings made to YHWH. This verse establishes a radical economic principle: one tribe lives entirely on the worship economy. The repetition of this statement in v. 33, framed even more starkly ("YHWH... is their inheritance"), brackets the entire Transjordan allotment with a theology of divine provision.

**V. 21-22 — Sihon's vassal chiefs and Balaam:** The parenthetical mention of the five Midianite chiefs (nesi'e Sikhon, "princes of Sihon") recalls Numbers 31:8. They are identified as vassals (nesikhe) of Sihon — local rulers who owed allegiance to the Amorite king. The mention of Balaam son of Beor, killed alongside them, is striking: the famous prophet-for-hire from Numbers 22-24 met his end not in a prophetic confrontation but in a military engagement. The text labels him ha-qosem — "the diviner" — a term of disapproval in Israelite usage. PNEUMA renders "diviner" to preserve the narrator's negative judgment.

**V. 27 — "Sea of Chinnereth" (yam Kinnereth):** The ancient name for the Sea of Galilee. The name may derive from kinnor (harp/lyre), perhaps describing the lake's shape. PNEUMA retains the Hebrew name as it appears in the source.

**V. 33 — "YHWH the God of Israel is their inheritance":** The chapter's final statement restates v. 14 with a critical intensification. In v. 14, the Levites' inheritance was the fire offerings — the sacrificial portions. Here it is YHWH himself. The shift from offerings to God is theologically significant: the Levites do not merely receive food from the altar; they receive a relationship with the one to whom the altar belongs. PNEUMA preserves both formulations exactly as the Hebrew gives them, without harmonizing.

**On the Vaticanus LXX:** The Greek text of Joshua 13 in Codex Vaticanus shows some significant variations from the Masoretic text. The list of unconquered territories in vv. 2-6 shows minor differences in geographic names and their ordering. The Vaticanus text tends to clarify ambiguous geographic references and occasionally adds explicative glosses. The Transjordan allotment sections follow the Hebrew closely in structure, though with characteristic Greek renderings of place-names (e.g., Seon for Sihon, Og for Og). The Levi statements in vv. 14 and 33 are preserved in both traditions, confirming their structural importance. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as primary source throughout.

— PNEUMA
